summary: Dan Shapiro traces the evolution of AI interfaces through four stages: completion, chat, agents, and 'claws' — autonomous agents that learn and act on your behalf without being asked. After an initial security disaster with OpenClaw and a failed attempt at running one in a locked-down sandbox, he found a middle path: giving his claw carefully scoped permissions with clear guardrails. The result was something qualitatively different from prior AI tools — an autonomous assistant that mines contacts, drafts emails, and does background research. He concludes by announcing plans to deploy claws across his company Glowforge, building what he calls a 'claw printer.'
key_points: Claws represent a fourth evolution beyond completion, chat, and agents: they are agents that learn and act autonomously without waiting to be asked
Full autonomy is dangerous and full lockdown is useless — the sweet spot requires carefully scoped permissions with clear guardrails and cleanup mechanisms
The key differentiator of claws isn't memory itself but the self-improvement loop that memory enables, allowing the system to take initiative toward your goals
Practical claw design means asymmetric permissions: read anything, but write only in controlled ways (drafts not sends, special folders, thumbprinted artifacts)
The natural next step is mass deployment — giving every person and department their own customized claw
money_quotes: Completion finishes your sentence. Chat discusses things with you. Agents work for you. Claws work without you.
A claw is an agent that learns and acts on your behalf without waiting for you to ask.
The more autonomy you give them, the more they get both helpful and dangerous. The reverse is also true.
It was safe. It was also useless. I built an extra-slow ChatGPT with a Mac Mini for a toupee.
I do not need new ways to send emails I regret. I have achieved sufficient coverage in that market.
I've gone from building a claw, to building a claw printer. Everyone deserves their own, custom claw.
I learned this the dumb way, which is my preferred curriculum.
key_insight: The next frontier beyond AI agents is autonomous 'claws' that learn and act on your behalf without prompting — but making them useful requires carefully scoped permissions rather than full autonomy or full lockdown.
